md: A **translation** slides every point of a shape the *same distance in the same direction*. Nothing rotates, nothing flips, nothing changes size — the shape just moves.
If a translation shifts a point right by 3 units and up by 2 units, then **every** point of the shape shifts right by 3 and up by 2. We write the rule as:
or more generally for any shift $(d_x, d_y)$:
The faded outline is the **pre-image** (original); the solid one is the **image** (the result of the transformation). Drag the $d_x$ and $d_y$ sliders — every vertex moves by exactly the same vector.

blocks.title: What a translation preserves
blocks.body:
Every transformation has a *signature* — what stays the same and what changes.

**Preserved by translation:** size, shape, orientation, angles, side lengths, parallel lines stay parallel.

**Changed by translation:** location only.

Because a translation preserves everything except position, the pre-image and image are always **congruent** (same shape and size). Translations are a kind of **rigid motion** (also called an **isometry**).

md: The pair $(d_x, d_y)$ that describes a translation is called the **translation vector**. You can also write it in column form $\begin{pmatrix} d_x \\ d_y \end{pmatrix}$ or with an arrow above it $\vec{v}$. Same idea, different notation.
The little prime mark ($A'$, read 'A prime') is the convention for the image of $A$.
Every translation can be undone by another translation. The **inverse** of a translation by $(d_x, d_y)$ is a translation by $(-d_x, -d_y)$.
Example: a translation by $(3, -2)$ followed by a translation by $(-3, 2)$ returns every point to where it started.
**Identity translation** = a translation by $(0, 0)$ — it does nothing.

blocks.title: Translating shapes — quick mental model
blocks.body: Pick the easiest vertex to compute (often a corner at the origin or with simple coordinates), apply the translation rule to it, then translate every other vertex by the same vector. The shape's interior comes along for the ride.

md: **Example.** The triangle $\triangle ABC$ has vertices $A(1, 1)$, $B(4, 1)$, $C(2, 3)$. Translate by $(d_x, d_y) = (-3, 2)$.
Triangle $A'B'C'$ is congruent to $\triangle ABC$, just shifted left 3 and up 2.
